Looking at the packets exchanged:

```
<< OPEN ... capabilities=[Multiprotocol(ipv4 unicast), Route Refresh, ASN4(41)]
```

This mean your PEER IS NOT CONFIGURED to accept VPN routes. ExaBGP will NOT SEND ON THE WIRES packets for AFI/SAFI paris (families) which are not negotiated part of the OPEN exchange.

In comparaison look at all the protocol ExaBGP is indicating it is willing to support
```
>> OPEN ... capabilities=[Multiprotocol(ipv4 unicast,ipv4 multicast,ipv4 nlri-mpls,ipv4 mpls-vpn,ipv4 flow,ipv4 flow-vpn,ipv6 unicast,ipv6 mpls-vpn,ipv6 flow,ipv6 flow-vpn,l2vpn vpls), ASN4(41)]
```

The key ones being `ipv4 nlri-mpls` and `ipv4 mpls-vpn`. without this in the OPEN packet of your peer, the only thing the BGP specification says is that you can send NORMAL 'ipv4 unicast/multicast' routes.

If you are still confused, please read some BGP tutorial on the net.

Good luck.

I found the issue. The incoming commands are added to RIB._modify_sorted through insert_announced method. The worker that is getting all the routes from RIB._modify_sorted and constructs the UPDATE message and sends it. Since our usage is pushing the commands during bootstrap faster than the worker could construct the message, the UPDATE message constructed leaves out some of the commands that were added after the enumerator started with dict_sorted.items(). However RIB._modify_sorted is cleared at the end of the enumeration that is causing the routes added after the enumeration start to be dropped and never announced to the peers. 

I have the fix that addressed this issue. I have moved the clearing of RIB._modify_sorted to be done just after getting the reference in dict_sorted. I will send out the pull request soon.

To mitigate this behavior, you need to expose sherpa on the host rather than via the routing mesh.  Swap out the ` -p 4550:4550 ` with `--publish mode=host,target=4550,published=4550` and it works like a charm.

- Any type you publicly expose through parameters, return values, or exceptions should be publicly exposed.

It's possible to still load a saved model if the model code has changed, but only if the weights in the model are the same.  It would be very difficult to allow the library to handle the case where the weights are different - there's too much variability in what could have changed, and how.

You _can_ however, write your own code to load your previous model and then copy the weights over to your new model.  You'll need two different `Model` classes, one that has the old code and gets loaded using `load_archive()`, and the other which gets initialized using an instance of the old model.  After you have this model, you can call our `train` commands without issue.  You can look at [the code for the `fine-tune` command](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/6044cceddd359a23f5bf7eb7ba895bec8e77fbb8/allennlp/commands/fine_tune.py#L115-L221) to see what this code should look like.

I added a test in that there commit 0664941. I also used the small MEI document in that test to find the problem.

Currently, the MEI importer puts Instrument objects in Measure objects, like this:
```
>>> zed.parts[0]
<music21.stream.Part 0x7f10731b2710>
>>> zed.parts[0].show('text')
{0.0} <music21.stream.Measure 1 offset=0.0>
    {0.0} <music21.instrument.Instrument 1: Clarinet: Clarinet>
    {0.0} <music21.clef.BassClef>
    {0.0} <music21.key.KeySignature of 1 flat, mode major>
    {0.0} <music21.meter.TimeSignature 8/8>
    {0.0} <music21.stream.Voice 1>
        {0.0} <music21.note.Note E>
```

It was probably through an inspection like this that I initially determined that instrument metadata is imported properly—you can see it right there!

However, I took a quick look in `Stream.getInstruments()` and discovered that it only looks one level deep for Instrument objects. Therefore the solution is to place the Instrument at the same level as the Measure, rather than inside.

Housekeeping...

I opened this ages ago when I was first getting involved with Deis.  At the time, what I had wanted was to get some assurance that _n_ application containers would be spread across racks or availability zones to ensure availability in the event of a zone-wide failure, for instance.

What I see more clearly a year later is that this doesn't matter so much.

1. This can't be done with Fleet anyway.
2. It's the scheduler's responsibility to reschedule containers if hosts fail.  Kubernetes replication controllers, for instance, are very good at this.
3. The most this would have accomplished is avoiding some small amount of downtime while Docker pulls complete on a machine to which a container has been re-assigned (if it didn't already have the image in question).
4. The possibility of downtime due to no. 3 is mitigated if you're running at any sort of scale.

At the end of the day, it's the _cluster's_ health and ability to host _n_ instances of you app that matter; _not_ which nodes of the cluster are doing it or how evenly the _n_ are spread over nodes in different racks or AZs.

I see no reason not to close this.

The short answer is that I have it working. It requires a cytools build from source.

The long answer:

The Python environment within AWS lambda is 3.6 and built WITHOUT --with-fpectl, but most environments are built with it. To build a proper lambda environment, here are the steps:

1. You need Ubuntu 17.04. It's the only official AMI that can support 3.6. The AWS Linux ones that exist for Lambda do not support 3.6. Go figure. You actually need to upgrade an Ubuntu 16.04 to 17.04. Fun.
2. Do a normal virtualenv configuration of your 3.6 environment.
3. BEFORE pip installing ethereum or web3.py, you need to manually build cytoolz.
- git clone https://github.com/pytoolz/cytoolz.git
- cd cytools
- git checkout release
- make
4. for file in VITUALENVROOT/lib/python3.6/site-packages/cytoolz/*; do cp -R cytoolz/`basename $file`VIRTUALENVROOT/lib/python3.6/site-packages/cytoolz/; done
5. pip install ethereum -y
6. pip install web3 -y
7. [if you are deploying new contracts] pip install -y py-solc
8. Package up your lambda

Building cytoolz on your Ubuntu system with figure it all out.

So as it stands (2) is essentially already in place, we just don't have a `G.embedding` attribute.
It's not a _problem_, but I do think that shuffling around the coordinates using calls to `G.set_coordinates(foo[:,slice])` is a little cumbersome. As for positives: (2) does solve an issue with keyword arguments for N-dimensional embeddings - setting the default slice. (2) also does not need any new code, and we won't have to change the dimension checking in `graphs.Graph.set_coordinates()`.

So (1) requires new plotting logic, new dimension checking logic in `set_coordinates`, and some decision on what the default should be (the obvious choice is [0,1,2]). On the other hand, the call to plot a specific set of dimensions is one line rather than 2.  If one wants to plot multiple views on an embedding, this does start to matter.  Finally, it does seem stylistically more organic to me to be able to slice with the plotting function - one can do this inline with `plt.scatter` after all, it just doesn't have the nice pygsp wrapping. 

I will note that adding the keyword arg (1) does not completely abrogate (2).

So almost half the time seems to be spent on regular expression searches. I did a separate,
more hacky experiment where I traced all function calls and that seems to back up this
conclusions (most calls are to re.compile and re.search). It looks to me like the main
culprit may be conda/core/package_cache.py:191(get_all_extracted_entries).

Here are the same top 30 functions with callee information. I got this by calling

```
pstats.Stats('conda.profile').sort_stats('cumulative').print_callees(30)
```

and then editing the output to remove any calls that cost little or no time and
shortening paths. That give a bit of a better picture for the code path. About
half the time is spent in intex.py(get_index), the other in package_cache.py(get_all_extracted_entries).

Within get_all_extracted_patterns about 2/3 of the time seems to go to
regular expression searches - 10 million of them in fact - plus some time
in safe_basename.

Within get_index, 2/3 of the time seem to go towards the url requests and
the rest to processing and writing pickled data.

We intentionally blocked this behavior because Classic registry has a dependency on a user storage account. The semantic of the following command is to (1) create a new storage account; and (2) create a registry.

```
az acr create -n Registry -g ResourceGroup --sku Classic
```

When you run the command the second time, it creates another storage account and `updates` your registry with the new storage account.

It can truly be idempotent if `--storage-account-name` is provided:

```
az acr create -n Registry -g ResourceGroup --sku Classic --storage-account-name mystorage
```

@tjprescott We could internally do a GET first to mock the idempotent behavior, then if the second command provides different parameters, I assume we should just update the resource?

`az webapp create` doesn't have a dependent resource and they make a PUT request regardless, which is idempotent.

I'd like to understand your usecase more before considering this.

Currently the framework is intentionally "take filenames, pass them to hooks" and doesn't really concern itself with what the hooks do.

That said, I personally think "linting partial diffs" isn't a reasonable goal and for the most part the current hook implementations agree ([one](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/blob/master/pre_commit_hooks/check_added_large_files.py) [two](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/blob/master/pre_commit_hooks/check_merge_conflict.py) exceptions -- but for _good_ reasons).  Most of my resistance comes from the following reasoning:

- Partial linting leads to inconsistency, new code doesn't look like old code which is almost worse
- Move the code? haha sucks to be you now you get to reformat it
- If someone bypasses the hooks (via `SKIP` or (hopefully not) `--no-verify`) then it is easy to sneak in new bad code
- What to do during commit of conflict resolution?  Lint all three places that could be introducing code?

I **do** understand the want to _incrementally_ introduce new linting -- and the framework gives you the ability to do that as long as you're opting in file-by-file (through either `files` or `exclude` (the latter is easier to incrementally apply as new files will be required to adhere by default).  And then you either fix file-by-file or directory-by-directory or however you want to approach it.

Curious to hear your thoughts on this!

Also note that `pygrep` is intended to be _dead simple_ so I'm also hesitant to add complexity there :)

So tl;dr is pylint does some static analysis and some dynamic analysis.  When put in an isolated virtualenv it does not work properly.  Using isolated virtualenvs achieves the design goal of reproducibility in pre-commit.

That said, there are still a few options for using pylint with pre-commit:

- disable the dynamic checking parts (not usually the desirable option)
- use it as a "system" hook (usually through "local" hooks).

You can find some related issues:
- https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/issues/362
- https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/issues/266
- https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/issues/178
- https://github.com/Yelp/venv-update/blob/de9e1befdecf448cf02ceb5c57a30bcc88a58c06/.pre-commit-config.yaml#L30-L36
- http://pre-commit.com/#repository-local-hooks

In [hotword.py](https://github.com/googlesamples/assistant-sdk-python/blob/master/google-assistant-sdk/googlesamples/assistant/library/hotword.py) you see

```python
with Assistant(credentials, device_model_id) as assistant:
    events = assistant.start()
    // ...
    for event in events
        process_event(event)
```

If you rewrite it to listen for Snowboy, and a hotword is activated, then you can do [start_conversation](https://developers.google.com/assistant/sdk/reference/library/python/#google.assistant.library.Assistant.start_conversation) like:

```python
with Assistant(credentials, device_model_id) as assistant:
    // Snowboy listen
    for snowboyEvent in snowboyListening:
        assistant.start_conversation()
        for event in events:
            process_event(event)
    // ...
```

It sounds like you're trying an import in two different installations of Python, or two different environments. Check `sys.executable` to see which Python and environment you're running in, and `sys.path` to see where it looks to import modules.

Python packages must be installed separately for each copy of Python you use, and if you are using virtualenvs or conda envs, packages must be installed into each environment where you need them. Either the package is not installed in one, or a different version of the package is installed.

To install packages to a particular Python installation or environment using pip, you can run it like this:

```shell
path/to/python -m pip install ...
# Replace path/to/python with the path of the Python executable (sys.executable)
```

If you are using conda environments, you can install packages from conda like this:

```shell
source activate myenv  # On Windows, just 'activate myenv' (no 'source')
conda install ...
```

But even if it did, it would then be impossible to distinguish `shift+a` from `shift+A`; one of those would have to be an invalid binding.

\* That's a deprecated API, but it's the only one which currently across all browsers. The keyboard API is probably the worst area for browser compatibility at the moment. The [DOM 3 standard](http://www.w3.org/TR/DOM-Level-3-Events/#widl-KeyboardEvent-key) (which fixes most of the issues) is not yet supported by Chrome.

I added some calls to `np.where` to avoid the division by zero, which arose from using the formula `g * np.conj(x) / np.abs(x)` for the reverse-mode Jacobian operator of the complex modulus function. This fix implements the definition that the derivative of the complex modulus function `lambda x: np.sqrt(x * np.conj(x))`, also known as `np.abs`, at zero is zero, which [seems to be the right definition at zero](https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/413405/derivative-of-absolute-value-over-the-complex-numbers). (Actually, I'm not sure where the formula  `g * np.conj(x) / np.abs(x)` came from, since I don't think `np.abs` is complex differentiable anywhere except zero, but I trust it was put there for a reason and I didn't change it.) The `np.fabs` function doesn't take complex arguments and so it just uses `g * np.sign(g)`, as you probably noticed.

New REST API.

This release of Blockstack introduces a new RESTful interface, which will become the basis for building decentralized applications.

The RESTful API fulfills the same responsibilities as a traditional Web app back-end.  Front-end code can use it to do things like create user accounts, query user profiles, load static resources, and read and write user data.  The programming model is otherwise the same as it is for normal Web apps.

Despite this similarity, the REST API brings several powerful features to apps behind the scenes that are not present in the current Web.

* **No passwords required**.  All authentication happens with public-key cryptography.  The sign-in experience for users is like signing in with Facebook or Google, except the blockchain (not a centralized corporation) is the shared trusted party.  At the same time, developers do not need to worry about asking for passwords, storing them securely, or coding ways for users to recover them.

* **No hosting required**.  The application does not need to store any user data, but users and developers get the same programming model and convenience of having a centralized app database.  To save data, the front-end only needs to upload data to the user's local Blockstack node via its REST API. The Blockstack node takes care of making it discoverable and replicating it to the user's storage providers.  To load it again, the front-end only needs to request it by path from the local Blockstack node (just like how it's done on the traditional Web); the local node will discover and read it from the user's storage servers automatically.

* **End-to-End Content Authenticity**.  Any app resource or piece of user data loaded through the REST interface is automatically verified against the origin's public key.  The content is guaranteed to come from the *person* who wrote it, so it doesn't matter which host served it.  This is a more powerful guarantee than TLS can offer, and Blockstack provides it by default (so users and developers don't have to muck with certificates).

* **API Permissions**.  Just like how mobile applications need to ask permission at install-time to access various APIs on the phone, all Blockstack applications need to ask permission to access various API endpoints.  This happens when the user creates an account with an application.  For example, a Dropbox-like application would need to ask permission to use the storage API, but not the name-registration API.  Users can also preemptively revoke permissions on existing accounts.

* **User introspection**.  In the traditional Web, users have to go re-enter the same profile information over and over again for each app they use.  With Blockstack, that information only has to be entered once (like with Gravatar or Facebook); applications (with permission) can query it automatically when the user makes an account with them.  In addition, users have the freedom to make different unlinkable personas for different sets of apps in order to selectively disclose information.

This was my original thought when I was trying to get `scrypt` distribution to work for OSX.

There's two downsides --

First, the `scrypt` backend in Cryptography only provides a KDF -- while we are using the simple `scrypt` encryption/decryption functions. So we'd have to re-implement [scryptenc.c:scryptenc_buf](https://github.com/Tarsnap/scrypt/blob/master/lib/scryptenc/scryptenc.c#L310) and the decryption function. Not so bad though.

The second, and bigger downside, is that `scrypt` in Cryptography requires linking with OpenSSL >= 1.1. This is fine for the OSX distribution, as the mac wheels in pip are statically linked with the latest OpenSSL. This is, however, problematic on many Linux distributions. In Ubuntu, for example, you'd need an unofficial PPA (even the next release 17.10 only includes OpenSSL 1.0.2g) or to build it yourself.

I think that the second problem outweighs the potential benefits of moving to Cryptography.

I've just run into a minor variation of the same pattern as in the OP:

```python
if True:
   foo = 1
   foo
   del foo
[foo for foo in ()]
```

> Ah, yeah. So there was another change, which I can't find now, which addressed this situation (going from memory here):
>
>     foo = "foo"
>     if False:
>       del foo
>     print foo
>
> This would complain about the final 'print foo' referencing an undefined foo, but as you can see, this code runs just fine.

If that was ever fixed, I think that fix should be reverted. No one should expect PyFlakes to be able to determine the truth value of an expression, and the warning must be present. Consider this:

```python
import random
foo = "foo"
if random.randint(0, 9) == 0:
  del foo
print foo
```

The above will fail one every 10 times. PyFlakes can't know which, and must warn, because that's a bug. An argument could be made about handling the special case of `if False:` and `if True:` and only taking the appropriate branch ignoring the other, but that's optional and it would not be a big deal if it didn't happen.

This fixes the issue with minimum changes. The problem i.e being fixed here is in regard to the node to which the `Argument` node is being associated.

Previously the `source` attribute of `Argument` node i.e `node.source` pointed to `FunctionDef` node resulting in incorrect `lineno` and `column_offset` of the argument. But now the source attribute points to the `arg` node thus associating arguments with correct lineno and col_offset. This fix is only for PY3 as `arg` node was introduced in here. Patch has no changes for PY2.

@brianmay You are right, it is indeed different. Contrary to normal UDP proxying, which still sends the packets to its original destination, only via the server instead of the client; DNS proxying captures the DNS packets and then uses whatever DNS servers are configured in the server and uses that as destination. That's why it works even when the DNS servers on the client are private and not acessible by server (they are not used really, instead the server uses its DNS servers and sends the response back).

My approach does not work because if the DNS servers are private to the client's network, when the packet gets to the server and it tries to send to those servers, it fails and nothing comes back.

> First of all, thanks for working on this API. By far the most usable solution i've found on the web so far. And the docs are pretty good too! This is not a bug, I'm just looking for help implementing something using your wrapper.

Hi!  Thanks, hopefully we can provide some assistance as well.

> I'm having some trouble with the acquire_cred_from() method. Which I see is an extension, but i'm wondering if you can help me. I've done a kinit as a user, and i'm hoping to use this method to acquire the cred and forward it to a remote host. Am I understanding this function correctly?

I think you probably want `init_sec_context()`, if I understand what you're trying to do correctly.  The normal workflow is that one end of a connection will call `init_sec_context()` and send the resulting payload to the other end, while the other end calls `accept_sec_context()` on the payload.

> In your documentation it says that the cred store needs to be a dict. Do you know what information needs to be in the dict?

We do a conversion of the Python dict to the format that the underlying GSSAPI layer expects without checking presence/absence of fields, since there are different ways to call this function.  Unfortunately this extension didn't get an RFC, so the best documentation available on what it can take seems to be [our test](https://github.com/pythongssapi/python-gssapi/blob/master/gssapi/tests/test_raw.py#L488-L517), or if you're more familiar with C, [krb5's test](https://github.com/krb5/krb5/blob/master/src/tests/gssapi/t_credstore.c) and [the thing that calls it](https://github.com/krb5/krb5/blob/f0c094a1b745d91ef2f9a4eae2149aac026a5789/src/tests/gssapi/t_gssapi.py#L83-L93).  Digging through the krb5 source, it appears that the possible ways to call here are None (which translates to GSS_C_NO_CRED_STORE) or as a dict possibly containing entries for any of: "ccache", "client_keytab", "keytab", or "rcache".  @DirectXMan12 since you wrote the code that interfaces with the cred store extension, do you have anything to add here?

> I have one other question, which is about the acquire_cred_with_password() function. It seems that when I use that function to acquire credentials, my password is stored in plain text in the credentials object. Is this expected behaviour?

We don't do any fancy handling on credentials objects, so I expect that it is.  However, this is probably not an issue since credentials are generally treated as secret: that is, one does not pass credentials around the network, to untrusted parties, etc. (see above for more information on how the handshake usually works).  If, on the other hand, you're concerned about processes having direct access to credentials, the [GSS-Proxy](https://fedorahosted.org/gss-proxy/) might be able to help.

Hope that helps!

This is something we also need for most projects. Currently we keep re-implemeting it in each project so I would like to work getting this in Oscar.

We have the following requirements:
 - Options which have the same price for all products
 - Options which have a different price per product (e.g. an installation fee).

We currently achieve this by setting a default price on the Option model and create a separate model (`OptionPrice`) which overrides the default price for a specific option/product combination.

The selected options are then passed to Strategy.fetch_for_product() where the strategy can calculate the combined price. 

This doesn't allow a different option price per partner or a stock for an option, but this can be achieved the same way I believe.

Let me know if this is something we would like to see in Oscar.

`/dev/ttyS0` of  Raspberry Pi 3 Model B does not support EVEN and ODD.
So, you need to use `/dev/ttyAMA0`.
However `/dev/ttyAMA0`  of  Raspberry Pi 3 Model B is bound to Bluetooth module.
In order to use `/dev/ttyAMA0`, you need to add the followings line into  `/boot/config.txt`.

```
dtoverlay=pi3-miniuart-bt
```

After reboot, you can use /dev/ttyAMA0 as physical serial port.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pvlib.pvsystem import singlediode, v_from_i, i_from_v, retrieve_sam

def fivepoints_to_frame(pnt):
    """
    Converts a 1 dimensional, dict-like singlediode or sapm result
    to a 5 row DataFrame with columns current and voltage.
    Users can iterate over the rows of a multidimensional
    singlediode or sapm result, if necessary.
    """
    ivframe = {'i_sc': (pnt['i_sc'], 0),
               'p_mp': (pnt['i_mp'], pnt['v_mp']),
               'i_x': (pnt['i_x'], 0.5*pnt['v_oc']),
               'i_xx': (pnt['i_xx'], 0.5*(pnt['v_oc']+pnt['v_mp'])),
               'v_oc': (0, pnt['v_oc'])}
    ivframe = pd.DataFrame(ivframe, index=['current', 'voltage']).T
    ivframe = ivframe.sort_values(by='voltage')

    return ivframe

resistance_shunt = 16
resistance_series = 0.094
nNsVth = 0.473
saturation_current = 1.943e-09
photocurrent = 7
module_parameters = retrieve_sam('cecmod')['Example_Module']

v_oc = v_from_i(resistance_shunt, resistance_series, nNsVth, 0, saturation_current, photocurrent)
voltage = np.linspace(0, v_oc, 100)

current = i_from_v(resistance_shunt, resistance_series, nNsVth, voltage, saturation_current, photocurrent)

fivepnts = singlediode(
    module_parameters, photocurrent, saturation_current, resistance_series, resistance_shunt, nNsVth)
ivframe = fivepoints_to_frame(fivepnts)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(voltage, current)
ax.scatter(ivframe['voltage'], ivframe['current'], c='k', s=36, zorder=10)
ax.set_xlim(0, None)
ax.set_ylim(0, None)
ax.set_ylabel('current (A)')
ax.set_xlabel('voltage (V)')

Is there a reason that polymorphic "list" endpoints aren't supported? 

There is an exception raised by the get_fields method of PolymorphicModelSerializer that just says "("Cannot get fields from a polymorphic serializer given a queryset")" 

Also in the tests there is a "test_polymorphism_on_polymorphic_model_list_post" where you can POST to a polymorphic endpoint, but there is no "test_polymorphism_on_polymorphic_model_list" (ie. no GET project-list)

I've patched this by removing the line that throws the exception in get_fields and then using the following (I changed the models to fit the Project example)

```
class PolyListSerializer(serializers.ListSerializer):
    _poly_force_type_resolution = True

class PolymorphicProjectSerializer(serializers.PolymorphicModelSerializer):
    polymorphic_serializers = [ArtProjectSerializer, ResearchProjectSerializer]

    class Meta:
        model = ProjectSerializer
        list_serializer_class = PolyListSerializer
```

This returns a list where the individual items are listed with the proper "type", unlike before where they were all listed with the parent type.

seems non-linear side-conditions are ignored during local optimization. Sorry, while I will try to address the soundness problem, you are unlikely to use Z3 in a constructive way when optimizing with non-linear integer constraints.

The SMT-LIB syntax is 

           ((_ at-most k) x y z u v), 
           ((_ at-least k) x y z u v), 
           ((_ pbge c1 c2 c3 c4 c5 k) x y z u v), 
           ((_ pble c1 c2 c3 c4 c5 k) x y z u v), 
           ((_ pbeq c1 c2 c3 c4 c5 k) x y z u v), 

If you only use bit-vectors and PB constraints, then set the logic to QF_FD (quantifier-free finite domains). It then uses the SAT solver.  

For example, you can write:

        (declare-const p_0_0 Bool)
        (declare-const p_0_1 Bool)
        (assert ((_ at-most 1)  p_0_0 p_0_1))
        (assert (or  p_0_0 p_0_1))
        (check-sat)
        (get-model)

> as long as PYTHONPATH is set up correctly, and libz3.dll is in the PATH (on Windows).

I do not know how the PYTHONPATH is managed on Windows, but in Linux packages are installed into directories below paths like ```/usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages```. PYTHONPATH then contains this directory. To make it possible to import all the packages inside this directory, they need to provide a ```__init__.py``` file as @hph86 provided (compare https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/modules.html#packages ). Setting the PYTHONPATH to each individual python package is then not reqired. However even with the __init__.py file, setting the PYTHONPATH to the directory containing the binary module would still work.

At the moment there seems to be no other way. Those paths have to be set up by someone, i.e., the ACIDE team or yourself, and at the moment the ACIDE team does not publish packages for OSX, so you'll have to do that yourself. I'm on Sander's side of not breaking backwards compatibility in a way that can't even be communicated to the user. Adding `epsilon=0` with some documentation would be nice, though.

> But if someone really cares about exact reproducibility they probably ought to fix a version of Theano + Lasagne at the start of their experiments and never upgrade.

What if I build on some previous experiments from half a year ago and want to use some of the more recent Lasagne features? Do I have to craft my own version of Lasagne for that by carefully merging in some of the changes and omitting others? Keeping things backwards-compatible (or break loudly if that's not possible) makes it a lot easier to upgrade Lasagne. We're doing a good job so far.

> Theano also has compatibility-breaking changes fairly often

I was under the impression that they also cared about not breaking the interface. Do you have some example?

> I doubt adding epsilon=0.0 will help many people though. Are there any drawbacks to nonzero epsilon besides compatibility?

The only other drawback is that somebody might be clipping the predictions already, or enforce predictions within (0,1) in some other way.
But I'd advocate for "explicit is better than implicit" -- you should clip the predictions consciously, not unknowingly because somebody decided it would be good for you. It would be great to set up a simple "NaN guide" to help users encountering NaNs quickly find possible causes and counter-measurements, though (#298).

> It was very difficult to debug the source of NaN gradient, until I created 2 similar models with similar weights in both Lasagne and Keras. The only different is their objectives function.

Sorry for the trouble -- but I think this is because Keras didn't document it, not because Lasagne didn't implement it. You probably weren't available that the predictions are clipped before you compared the implementations in detail, and you probably wouldn't have mentioned it in a research paper describing your experiments either, would you?

> There is only 1 initial release and you guys have releases enormous amount of changes and news into the library.

Yes, that's something we're guilty of. We're not in a "release early, release often" mode, but careful to not introduce any changes in a release that have to be taken back. In addition, all core developers use the bleeding-edge version of Lasagne and Theano so there's not a lot of pressure to do a release (it's the same problem for Theano, I assume). Sorry!

The argument for this is that when we run nbval on https://github.com/computationalmodelling/nbval/blob/master/issues/7/tutorial_lib.ipynb, we effectively have tests that execute all the code in https://github.com/computationalmodelling/nbval/blob/master/issues/7/lib.py You may have thought further already, and there are some design questions to be asked and answered. For example, we could call the code in `lib.py` from the notebook, but we may not inspect the return value at all in the notebook. For example: 
a = lib.myprod(1, 2)
Running this inside a notebook through nbval testing would be a much weaker test than is in the demonstration at https://github.com/computationalmodelling/nbval/blob/master/issues/7/tutorial_lib.ipynb where we actually check that `a` has the right value. If the statement above (`a = lib.myprod(1,2)`) is tested via nbval and passes, it would basically confirm that `myprod()` exists in `lib` as a callable object, takes two arguments, and doesn't raise exceptions. Should this 'weaker test' count as being 'covered by a test'? That's the question. For our use case, I would say: 'yes': Having this minimal cover in place is a lot better than None. It also avoids the question how weak or strong a test needs to be to contributing to the coverage. But others may disagree. As a first approximation, I would propose that all the code that is executed through the commands in the input cells of the notebook should count as the coverage of that notebook. I haven't thought long about this though - other ideas and thoughts would be useful.

this makes a list with the content of e.g. 
`names = [u'usr', u'mnt', u'lib64', u'sbin', u'dev', u'proc', '\x01\xa1']`

sorted(names)

```
names = self.fs.listdir(_sel_dir)
for item in names:
    _item = fs.path.combine(_sel_dir, item)

        names.remove(item)
```

Your server code looks it is blocking that's the reason why it... blocks. You should not have to change the default heartbeat. To me it looks like you are changing it to some high value because you are blocking the whole python process during the wget download.

Your code must be gevent compatible, and never block. One easy workaround in your case would be to run the wget code inside its own thread, and waiting on the completion via a gevent queue. This would keep the iolopp on the main thread running while the wget process is blocked. You can even use a [threadpool](http://www.gevent.org/gevent.threadpool.html) for this.

Of course the best solution would be to have a gevent compatible ([random example](https://github.com/kennethreitz/grequests)) version of your wget download.

We've had some rumblings of a resurgence of the idea recently, like what you're proposing. A recent one that we brainstormed, for example, would be:

- Make the KB an actual database (conceptually) that only _stores data_ (i.e., stuff like jump targets, block addresses, function addresses).
- Make a set of KB _views_ that interpret that data into a pythonic interface (i.e., stuff like `Function`, `Block`, etc).
- Have analyses put data in the KB and register KB Views.

What do you think of something like this? It's not too dissimilar to your proposal, but without `CFGProcessor` (which I think would be implemented as an Analysis baseclass in this design).

And just to think ahead: I see two applications of this:

1) motion detection. For motion detection from cross-correlation techniques you need two grids: the target grid which is the grid locations where you will have your motion vectors on, and the imaging grid, which is much finer resolution, and is where you reproject your satellite images. The motion algorithms are much easier to write if the two grids are exactly in line, but the imaging grid has finer resolution.

2) anti-aliasing in pyresample: here the idea is to instantiate a finer grid than the target area_def, do the pyresample against the finer grid, and finally collect the resampled output from the finer grid to the original target grid (simple average).

You can see our (very hacky) way of checking whether it's Spark 2.X or not here (spark variable presence means it's 2.X). Feel free to refactor the check so that you can use it if you need to: https://github.com/jupyter-incubator/sparkmagic/blob/f65571a3fb9092afbc03212587ce0710ed79cadf/sparkmagic/sparkmagic/livyclientlib/livysession.py#L138

As for escaping things properly in all languages, I would just create a JSON payload and let JSON libraries do the serialization/deserialization in all languages, similar to how we do auto visualization today. I wouldn't try to escape things manually. That way, you ensure all strings are supported, regardless of quote types or other factors. Note that we use jsonlite on the R kernel: https://github.com/jupyter-incubator/sparkmagic/blob/f65571a3fb9092afbc03212587ce0710ed79cadf/sparkmagic/sparkmagic/livyclientlib/sqlquery.py#L112

As for what needs to be done, we had this list. Feel free to disagree with me on which items should be on the list, and we can discuss. Once we get to the final list, you can check box what you've done so far to keep a tally of it, and once you've completed it, I will take a final review:

- [ ] escaping JSON right in all languages
- [ ] JSON escaping failure handling so user gets an actionable message
- [ ] handle multiple encodings correctly (in python 2 vs python3, for example)
- [ ] a way to limit the amount of data you can send. This means that a user can choose to send X rows from the pandas DF or all rows, or a sample, or etcetera. This is similar to the way users can currently choose how much data to visualize automatically from a dataframe.
- [ ] support for different versions of spark (2.X onwards at least) -- maybe 2.2 onwards for R...
- [ ] support for magic-based ipython kernel scenarios
- [ ] unit tests
- [ ] a spark to livy to sparkmagic version table to the README
- [ ] documentation in README on how to use this new feature
- [ ] documentation in sample notebooks on how to use this new feature to:
- [ ] generate a pandas df locally, serialize it to the cluster and then pass it to the remote spark cluster
- [ ] read a local file containing a serialized trained model into a string, pass it to the remote cluster, and deserialize there

What do you think? Sounds good? Please do reach out if you need to speak to me or if you need anything else.

Hi @gravityrail , Thanks for letting us know about this issue.
Your PR is appreciated, however your changes alone will break the current playback implementation, and without supporting backward compatibility, it will break past recording.
The record/playback module uses 3 files for de/serializing with rosbag:
 - `media/ros/ros_reader.h`
 - `media/ros/ros_writer.h`
 - `media/ros/ros_file_format.h`
The first is for deserializing, the second if for serializing and the third acts as a common data and functionality header.
We need to have the following complete to support this change:
1. Update `ros_file_format.h` to a produce the names of all topics to not have spaces. Probably only update the `convert` functions and `ros_topic::*` functions.
    This will make the writer and reader work without spaces (without changing them, I believe - need to verify)
2. Update the file_version to 4
3. Update the `ros_reader.h` to support all versions up to 4 (including)

If you feel like contributing, feel free to update this PR with changes and I'll review and assist.
Otherwise, let's open an issue and mark it as bug so we can track this and we will take care of it.

First, you'll need to validate whether or not the EnglishTokenizer is sufficient. I would do this by creating spanish versions of the examples and playing with them. Specifically, the tokenizer is punctuation aware and splits an utterance (sentence or phrase) into individual tokens (usually words). 

If the english tokenizer does not work well, you'll need to look for an equivalent to the Porter Stemmer algorithm for Spanish and implement it. The latter can be picked up by someone else, if that's beyond your scope. Validating whether or not the existing tokenizer is sufficient is a great first step.

It is true that 'batch_size' was not passed on and 'batch_size' controls the number of records being inserted at once. If not mentioned, [default batch_size 999 is used](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/ref/models/querysets/#bulk-create). However, it won't block batch insertion. 

Please check for [database error](https://github.com/pyexcel/pyexcel-io/blob/master/pyexcel_io/database/django.py#L74) such as integrity error during bulk import. Once there is any exception, it will default to 'snail' alike insertion - one record at a time, skipping offending ones.

I think what you are missing is that a header is always part of a signature, so just signing a random value will never give yo a valid "jws" signature:

From RFC7515:
   JWS Signing Input
      The input to the digital signature or MAC computation.  Its value
      is ASCII(BASE64URL(UTF8(JWS Protected Header)) || '.' ||
      BASE64URL(JWS Payload)).

So even if you ahve an empty header, at the very least you need to prepend a '.' character to generate a valid JWS signature.

From RFC 7518:
```
6.4.1.  "k" (Key Value) Parameter

   The "k" (key value) parameter contains the value of the symmetric (or
   other single-valued) key.  It is represented as the base64url
   encoding of the octet sequence containing the key value.
```

Therefore JWK will never accept as valid a bytestring, you have to convert it tio a base64url encoded string first.

Do you remember if you checked if `/etc/letsencrypt/live/xxxxxxxx.com/fullchain.pem` had actually been renewed or if Certbot just said it was? There's a known issue causing the behavior you're describing (see  #3497) but none of the four files in `/etc/letsencrypt/live/xxxxxxxx.com` are updated.

So for whatever reason your editor has added a lot of 'non-breaking space' characters instead of spaces to all of these files.
This unfortunately really breaks python - which expects all indentation to be done in terms of spaces.
I've prepared a patch that should change all of the broken whitespace characters back to regular space characters. It's included here as a .txt file. To apply it you should download it, change the file extension to .patch and then follow these instructions: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/2249852/how-can-i-apply-a-patch
Alternately you can go through in vi and change them back using the command `s/ / /g` with all of the text highlighted, but this is somewhat laborious.
Once you do that I'll leave some more comments on where the formatting still needs fixing, but that will help a lot.

[0001-fix-whitespaces.txt](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/files/1577196/0001-fix-whitespaces.txt)

There are 2 types of "license.lic", one is used by Pyarmor, the other is used by your product after it's obfuscated by Pyarmor. 

The former is located in the src path of Pyarmor, here it's `/home/awagner/miniconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pyarmor/license.lic`

If it's trial license, the key used to obfuscate the scripts are fixed, that is to say, all the obfuscated scripts use same key in Pyarmor trial version. After it's replaced with purchased license, the key used to obfuscate python scripts is random. And this license is not required when to run or import obfuscated sciprts.

The latter is generated by Pyarmor, it's required to run or import obfuscated scripts. It'll generated when obfuscating your product, and saved in the same path of your obfuscated product.

DO NOT ship your purchased license to your customer, only ship the "license.lic" generated by Pyarmor.

The optimizations at a high level:

 * We can only remove "z" if the "pen" is at the same position as the
   start of the path (base case "m0 0z", but theoretically you can have
   steps in between "mX Y" and "z" as long as "z").

 * We can only rewrite m0 0 X Y to lX Y if 1) there is no "z" following
   the "m", OR 2) there is an "m" between this "m" and the "z".
   - This is because "z" is "relative" to the latest "m" before it.
   - Note that you can have multiple "z" cmds after the same "m"
     (a la: "m0 0 10 10-10-10 z l 10-10-10 10 z")

 * We want to remove redundant "m0 0" at the end of the path (e.g.
   because we optimized out a trailing "z").

In my code, I have used a "reverse iteration" (i.e. start at the end of
the path and move towards the start).  This has the advantage that we
can optimize path nodes immediately when we see them (assuming the
conditions are right).
  In a forward iteration, you find a node and have to look forward
because you can determine whether you can delete it.

OK, here is the updated plan (implementation):

1. When sos is started, a task manager thread (or process) is created. This thread should tolerate multiple task manager from multiple SoS instances or from nested instances, and it will die when sos quits (no daemon process will be left).

2. When a step encounters a task, it either submit all tasks or one by one depending on `concurrent` setting.

3. The task will notify SoS (executor) that it is waiting for the completion of a list of jobs (send `Task` instances).

4. SoS will send the tasks to task manager and mark the step as `pending` (not `running`). The step process will be put aside (and not taking quota of `-j`).

5. Executor will continue and see if there are other steps that could be run, and run it.

6. At a new loop, SoS asks task manager if there are completed jobs. If there is, a step could resume execution, but it could send another task and falls asleep again.

7. If all tasks have been submitted and no more step could be run, and if `-w` is not specified, sos will quit with a list of submitted jobs.

8. Otherwise, sos would wait for the completion of steps and/or tasks.

For the task themselves,

1. The task will have task content (task_id and file), and have a instance of `Task`, which knows how to check for the status of jobs for different types of task queues.

With the new `.task` file format, tasks store signatures within the completed `.task` file. There is therefore no longer a concern on small signature files. The only use of 

```
task: sig_mode='ignore'
```
would be skipping the calculation of md5 of input and output files, at the cost of not able to skip a task, which is not important for production runs. However, I would argue in the later case a workflow level `-s ignore` would suffice so there is no need for task-specific signature mode.

So I think this will be ` WONTFIX` unless there is another strong argument for it.

Here is the script I used to test signatures. Please run it with options like

```
$ python test.py --dir ~/sos/sos -j 5
It took 5 processes  2.9 seconds (21.7 per 10000 files) to process 1353 files under 242 directories
```
to see if you can reproduce deadlocks.

```
from sos.targets import file_target, path
import argparse
import multiprocessing
import time
import os
import glob

def write_signature(root, dirs, files):
    #print(f'processing {len(files)} under {root}')
    for file in files:
        file_target(os.path.join(root, file)).write_sig()
    return len(files)


def testWriteSignature(dir, njobs):
    sig_file = os.path.join(os.path.expanduser('~'), '.sos', 'target_signatures.db')
    if os.path.isfile(sig_file):
        os.remove(sig_file)

    pool = multiprocessing.Pool(njobs)
    res = pool.starmap_async(
            write_signature, os.walk(dir))
    return res.get()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument('-d', '--dir', help='directory to generate signature')
    parser.add_argument('-j', '--jobs', type=int, help='number of processes')
    args = parser.parse_args()

    st = time.time()
    res = testWriteSignature(args.dir, args.jobs)
    print(f'It took {args.jobs} processes {time.time() - st : .1f} seconds ({(time.time() - st)*10000/sum(res):.1f} per 10000 files) to process {sum(res)} files under {len(res)} directories')



```

A few observations on what you're seeing (I plan to record audio over the top of it, and add it to https://github.com/dask/dask-examples eventually)

- Each bar in the UI is a single core. We seem to have good parallelism within each generation. The communication that does happen (in red) is I believe mostly just scores (which are small)
- There's a "straggler" problem in some generations (generation 3 is most noticeable I think), where one of the estimators, GradientBoostingClassifer in this case, takes much longer than the rest so all but a few cores of our cluster are idle. I've seen references to timeouts in TPOT, but haven't investigated if they work well here.
- There are decent gaps between generations, where the cluster is idle and just the client machine is doing computation. I assume this is the random mutation and cross over computation? I don't know how easy it would be to parallelize these blocks.

I'm closing this bug out. In PR #250 we determined this issue was caused by not having the VC++ runtime (and the relevant Windows updates) installed on the machine. https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink-chocolatey/issues/1 will cover adding the dependency to the chocolatey package.

For anyone in the future running into this issue, I would try to following steps:

1. Uninstall Streamlink
2. Install the Visual C++ 2015 redistributable
3. Restart the machine
4. Install Streamlink

If those steps don't solve your problem, then I would imagine something else has gone wrong and you should file a separate bug.

Hi there,

Many thanks for the detailed feedback, much appreciated. In general, I would agree with your points. 

However, I think it is important to understand the reasoning behind having these features.

The point is for the client side code to be able to say:

```
client.run_command('touch /root/blah', sudo=True)
```

And have the command executed correctly with sudo. Per documentation, the `sudo` keyword argument passes the command string to the `sudo` command, subject to quoting in order to execute correctly. There is always the option of not using the sudo keyword and writing a complete command string client side.

The expectation from the client's POV is that anything that works sans sudo will work with it. Meaning this:

```
client.run_command('for i in 1 2 3; echo $i; done')
```

and this:

```
client.run_command('for i in 1 2 3; echo $i; done', sudo=True)
```

Need to
* have same output
* not need different command string for sudo and no sudo
* not have to quote or escape command string for sudo when it is not required for non-sudo

This enables ease of use for the vast majority of use cases that are 

* Using a POSIX  shell
* Want to run their command with sudo

There are of course edge cases where this is not suitable, like non-posix shells, embedded devices etc. For those use cases, it is best to write a command string client side and use `use_shell=False` for paramiko and not use either sudo/user arguments for native client. 

As these features are useful to a large majority of users, myself included, it would not be appropriate to remove them. If they are not suitable for a particular use case, best to not use them.

For the specific issues raised:
1. Paramiko quoting.

Yes, this is true. I think the best thing would be to have it match behaviour with the native client and only quote the command string when running with `sudo`. This will mean `use_shell` can be removed. The quoting currently being used is accurate and is integration tested - see [variable substitution](https://github.com/ParallelSSH/parallel-ssh/blob/master/tests/test_pssh_client.py#L675), [backticks](https://github.com/ParallelSSH/parallel-ssh/blob/master/tests/test_pssh_client.py#L793), [escaped commands](https://github.com/ParallelSSH/parallel-ssh/blob/master/tests/test_pssh_client.py#L813), [single quotes](https://github.com/ParallelSSH/parallel-ssh/blob/master/tests/test_pssh_client.py#L779) etc tests.

2. Separate functions for bourne/posix shells.

No, I am not in favour or adding separate functions for posix/bourne shells. Can already send command strings as-is with the native client which is preferred. The by-default command escaping of the paramiko client will be removed as per 1.

3. "this essentially makes use of those arguments incompatible with any devices or configurations that do not support Bourne-compatible quoting syntax, or that only have su and not sudo"

That is ok. Again, these are convenience features for the majority of use cases they are suited to. Best not to use them otherwise. If you know you do not have sudo, need to do something special to use sudo or prefer to use `su` can always generate and/or escape the command string needed client side.

4. "If sudo is specified and shell is not, you should pass -s to sudo"

That breaks shell variables, multiple dependent commands, quoting, backticks, sub shells etc etc. See `sudo(8)` man page.

```
sudo -s for i in 1 2 3; echo $i; done
bash: syntax error near unexpected token `done'
sudo -s 'for i in 1 2 3; echo $i; done'
/bin/bash: for i in 1 2 3; echo ; done: command not found
sudo -s "for i in 1 2 3; echo $i; done"
/bin/bash: for i in 1 2 3; echo ; done: command not found
```

Example from man page:
```
sudo -s cd ~; ls -lh | sort -r > listing
(cd is not applied to rest of commands)
sudo sh -c "cd ~; ls -lh | sort -r > listing"
(this works correctly)
```

5. "It is not necessary to have sudo use a shell to run the command at all"

Does not work for anything that is not a simple command string - see above.

6. Inconsistent behaviour. 

Yes, this will be resolved. From further testing, there is no inconsistent behaviour in paramiko that requires quoting/escaping as long as sudo is not used.

Regarding your comment about adding custom code to generator output. I am no fan of adding manual content to generated files, as usually this leads to issues. Just think about how to add such content to GIT. You'd be checking in generated content as well in this case.

Therefore, manual code should really always be in a separate file. This is one of the reasons C# came up with the `partial` keyword, allowing the developer to write code in a separate file extending the class generated from UI wizards.

In Python I'd follow a similar route: Add a generator option that results in mixin classes being used in the generated code. These classes can then be implemented in a user-controlled module.

> I didn't get your last comment 

In your code, the NULL token is called `__NULL2`, which I presume you copied from the resulting parse tree. This is because anonymous terminals in Lark are named as `meaningful description if possible + automatic counter`. The counter exists to prevent collisions. However, it depends on the order in which the terminals appear in the grammar. If you change the order, you might find it become `__NULL3`, and so on.

I forgot to mention a simple solution for words cutting out in the middle: the `\b` modifier.

You can use it like this:

    NAME: /\b/ WORD /\b/

To make `NAME` only match whole words, instead of fragments.
